nodes.name: World Capitals
nodes.description: Capital cities of countries around the world

items.prompt: What is the capital of France?
items.explanation:
Population ~2.1 million (city proper), ~12 million (metro area)
Built on the Seine River; the Île de la Cité is the historic heart (Notre-Dame, Sainte-Chapelle)
Host city of the 2024 Summer Olympics
The Eiffel Tower (1889) was built as a temporary exhibit for the World's Fair and nearly demolished
France is the world's most visited country; Paris alone draws ~30 million tourists annually

items.prompt: What is the capital of the United Kingdom?
items.explanation:
Population ~9 million (city proper) — largest city in Western Europe
Seat of the British monarchy since the Norman Conquest (1066)
The City of London (the 'Square Mile') is one of the world's oldest financial centers
The Great Fire of 1666 destroyed most of medieval London; St. Paul's Cathedral was rebuilt by Christopher Wren
The Underground ('the Tube'), opened 1863, was the world's first underground railway

items.prompt: What is the capital of Germany?
items.explanation:
Population ~3.7 million; divided into East and West from 1945 to 1990
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989 — reunification followed on October 3, 1990
Brandenburg Gate, built 1791, became the symbol of German reunification
West Germany's capital was Bonn from 1949–1990; the government moved back to Berlin in 1999
Home to Museum Island, a UNESCO World Heritage Site with five museums on a Spree River island

items.prompt: What is the capital of Italy?
items.explanation:
Population ~2.8 million; founded (by legend) in 753 BCE by Romulus and Remus
Contains Vatican City, an independent city-state and the smallest country in the world (121 acres)
The Colosseum (c. 80 CE) could seat ~50,000 spectators for gladiatorial games
Capital of the Roman Republic, Roman Empire, and the Papal States before becoming Italy's capital in 1871
The Trevi Fountain collects ~3,000 euros in coins daily, donated to charity

items.prompt: What is the capital of Spain?
items.explanation:
Population ~3.3 million; located almost exactly at the geographic center of the Iberian Peninsula
Became the capital in 1561 under Philip II, replacing Valladolid
Home to the Prado Museum, one of the world's finest art collections (Velázquez, Goya, El Greco)
Highest-altitude capital in the EU at ~650 meters (2,130 ft) above sea level
Real Madrid and Atlético Madrid are two of Europe's top football clubs

items.prompt: What is the capital of Russia?
items.explanation:
Population ~13 million — the largest city in Europe
The Kremlin complex is the seat of the Russian government and a UNESCO World Heritage Site
St. Basil's Cathedral (1561) on Red Square, with its colorful onion domes, is Russia's most recognizable landmark
St. Petersburg was the capital from 1712–1918 under the tsars; the Bolsheviks moved it back to Moscow
The Moscow Metro (opened 1935) is famous for its ornate, palace-like stations

items.prompt: What is the capital of China?
items.explanation:
Population ~21 million; name means 'Northern Capital' in Mandarin
Home to the Forbidden City, the imperial palace for 24 emperors across the Ming and Qing dynasties
Tiananmen Square is one of the largest public squares in the world
Hosted the 2008 Summer Olympics and the 2022 Winter Olympics — first city to host both
The Great Wall's nearest sections to Beijing (Badaling, Mutianyu) are the most visited

items.prompt: What is the capital of Japan?
items.explanation:
Greater Tokyo metro area population ~37 million — the world's most populous metropolitan area
Originally called Edo; renamed Tokyo ('Eastern Capital') in 1868 when the emperor moved from Kyoto
Hosted the 1964 Summer Olympics (first in Asia) and the postponed 2020 Olympics (held in 2021)
The Tsukiji/Toyosu fish market is the world's largest wholesale fish and seafood market
Tokyo Tower (1958) and Tokyo Skytree (2012, 634m) are the city's iconic landmarks

items.prompt: What is the capital of India?
items.explanation:
Population ~1.4 million (New Delhi proper); Delhi metro area ~32 million
Designed by British architects Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker, inaugurated in 1931
Replaced Calcutta (now Kolkata) as capital of British India in 1911
India Gate, a war memorial, anchors the ceremonial boulevard Rajpath (now Kartavya Path)
New Delhi is technically a district within the National Capital Territory of Delhi

items.prompt: What is the capital of South Korea?
items.explanation:
Population ~9.7 million (city), ~26 million (metro area)
Served as the capital of Korean kingdoms for over 600 years (since the Joseon dynasty, 1394)
The DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) separating North and South Korea is just 35 miles north of Seoul
Gangnam District became globally famous from Psy's 'Gangnam Style' (2012), the first YouTube video to hit 1 billion views
Home to Samsung, Hyundai, and LG headquarters — South Korea's GDP per capita rivals Western Europe

items.prompt: What is the capital of Indonesia?
items.explanation:
Population ~10.5 million (city), ~34 million (metro area) — one of the world's most densely populated cities
Located on the northwest coast of Java, the world's most populous island
Indonesia is moving its capital to Nusantara on Borneo, announced in 2022, to relieve Jakarta's congestion and sinking
Jakarta is sinking up to 25 cm per year due to groundwater extraction — among the fastest-sinking cities on Earth
Fourth most populous country in the world with ~275 million people across 17,000+ islands

items.prompt: What is the capital of Brazil?
items.explanation:
Population ~3 million; a planned city inaugurated in 1960, replacing Rio de Janeiro as capital
Designed by urban planner Lúcio Costa and architect Oscar Niemeyer; shaped like an airplane from above
A UNESCO World Heritage Site — the largest 20th-century city to receive the designation
Rio de Janeiro was the capital from 1763–1960; before that, Salvador from 1549–1763
Built in 41 months in the interior cerrado (savanna) to promote development away from the coast

items.prompt: What is the capital of Mexico?
items.explanation:
Population ~9 million (city), ~21 million (metro area) — the largest city in North America
Built on the ruins of Tenochtitlan, the Aztec capital conquered by Cortés in 1521
Sits at 2,240 meters (7,350 ft) elevation in the Valley of Mexico, surrounded by volcanic mountains
Sinking at ~50 cm per year due to clay soil compression from groundwater extraction
The Zócalo (Plaza de la Constitución) is one of the largest public squares in the world

items.prompt: What is the capital of Argentina?
items.explanation:
Population ~3 million (city), ~15 million (metro area)
Known as the 'Paris of South America' for its European-style architecture and café culture
Birthplace of tango, which originated in the working-class neighborhoods of La Boca and San Telmo
Casa Rosada (the Pink House) is the executive mansion and seat of the Argentine president
Eva Perón ('Evita') remains the city's most famous figure; her tomb in Recoleta Cemetery is a major attraction

items.prompt: What is the capital of the United States?
items.explanation:
Population ~670,000; not a state — a federal district established by the Residence Act of 1790
Designed by Pierre Charles L'Enfant with a grid of lettered and numbered streets and diagonal avenues
The White House, Capitol Building, and Supreme Court are all within 2 miles of each other on the National Mall
D.C. residents pay federal taxes but had no presidential vote until the 23rd Amendment (1961)
New York City was the first U.S. capital (1789–1790), then Philadelphia (1790–1800)

items.prompt: What is the capital of Canada?
items.explanation:
Population ~1 million (metro area); located in Ontario on the Ontario-Quebec border
Chosen as capital by Queen Victoria in 1857 — a compromise between Toronto, Montreal, Kingston, and Quebec City
The Rideau Canal (UNESCO World Heritage Site) becomes the world's largest skating rink in winter (~7.8 km)
Parliament Hill overlooks the Ottawa River; the Centre Block's Peace Tower is 92 meters tall
The most common wrong answer for Canada's capital is Toronto (the largest city) or Montreal

items.prompt: What is the capital of Australia?
items.explanation:
Population ~460,000; a planned city chosen as a compromise between rivals Sydney and Melbourne
Designed by American architects Walter Burley Griffin and Marion Mahony Griffin, who won an international competition
Construction began 1913; Parliament House opened 1988 (the old Parliament House operated 1927–1988)
Melbourne served as the interim national capital from 1901–1927
The most common wrong answer is Sydney (largest city) — Canberra is only Australia's 8th-largest city

items.prompt: What is the capital of Saudi Arabia?
items.explanation:
Population ~7.6 million; located in the center of the Arabian Peninsula on the Najd plateau
The Al Saud family captured Riyadh in 1902, launching the campaign that created Saudi Arabia in 1932
Home to the Kingdom Centre Tower (302m), one of the tallest skyscrapers in the Middle East
Jeddah is the second city and gateway for Hajj pilgrims to Mecca
Riyadh means 'gardens' in Arabic, referring to the historical oasis settlement

items.prompt: What is the administrative capital of South Africa?
items.explanation:
South Africa has three capitals: Pretoria (executive), Cape Town (legislative), Bloemfontein (judicial)
Pretoria is commonly given as 'the' capital in trivia; it houses the president and government ministries
The Union Buildings in Pretoria are the official seat of the South African government
Named after Voortrekker leader Andries Pretorius; now part of the Tshwane metropolitan municipality
Nelson Mandela was inaugurated as president at the Union Buildings on May 10, 1994

items.prompt: What is the capital of Turkey?
items.explanation:
Population ~5.7 million; became the capital in 1923 when Atatürk founded the Republic of Turkey
Chosen over Istanbul to symbolize a break from the Ottoman Empire and its coastal vulnerability
Anıtkabir, Atatürk's mausoleum, is the city's most visited landmark
Istanbul is far larger (~16 million) and more famous, making Ankara one of trivia's classic 'trick' capitals
Ankara has been continuously settled since the Bronze Age; known as Ancyra in antiquity

items.prompt: What is the capital of Egypt?
items.explanation:
Population ~10 million (city), ~22 million (metro area) — largest city in the Arab world and Africa
Founded in 969 CE by the Fatimid dynasty; the name Al-Qahira means 'The Victorious'
The Great Pyramids of Giza are technically in Giza, across the Nile, but within Cairo's metro area
Al-Azhar University (founded 970 CE) is one of the oldest continuously operating universities in the world
Tahrir Square was the center of the 2011 Egyptian revolution that toppled Hosni Mubarak

items.prompt: What is the capital of Nigeria?
items.explanation:
Population ~3.6 million; became the capital in 1991, replacing Lagos
A planned city in the center of Nigeria, chosen for its central location and to avoid ethnic associations
Lagos remains by far the largest city (~15 million) and economic hub
The Nigerian National Mosque and National Church sit across from each other, symbolizing religious coexistence
One of trivia's frequent 'wrong answer' capitals — most people guess Lagos

items.prompt: What is the capital of Kenya?
items.explanation:
Population ~4.4 million; founded in 1899 as a railway depot on the Uganda Railway
Nairobi National Park is the only national park within a major capital city — lions with skyscrapers in the background
Serves as the headquarters of the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)
Kenyatta International Convention Centre is the city's iconic landmark
The name comes from the Maasai phrase 'enkare nairobi' meaning 'cool water'

items.prompt: What is the capital of Ethiopia?
items.explanation:
Population ~5.4 million; name means 'New Flower' in Amharic
Headquarters of the African Union (AU) since its founding as the OAU in 1963
Sits at ~2,355 meters (7,726 ft) elevation — the world's third-highest capital city
Lucy, the 3.2-million-year-old Australopithecus afarensis fossil, is housed at the National Museum
Ethiopia was never colonized (except for a brief Italian occupation 1936–1941)

items.prompt: What is the capital of Morocco?
items.explanation:
Population ~580,000; located on the Atlantic coast at the mouth of the Bou Regreg river
Casablanca is larger (~3.7 million) and more famous — a common trivia trap
The Hassan Tower, an incomplete 12th-century minaret, is Rabat's most recognizable landmark
Rabat's medina and modern city together form a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Became the capital under the French protectorate in 1912, replacing the traditional capital Fez

items.prompt: What is the capital of Myanmar?
items.explanation:
Population ~1.2 million; replaced Yangon (Rangoon) as the capital in 2005
A purpose-built city in the center of the country; the move was announced with almost no warning
Notorious for its enormous, largely empty 20-lane highways and vast government complexes
Yangon (~5 million) remains the largest city and economic center
One of trivia's hardest capitals — most people still think Yangon/Rangoon is the capital

items.prompt: What is the capital of Thailand?
items.explanation:
Population ~10.5 million (metro area); Bangkok's ceremonial name in Thai is 168 letters long — the world's longest place name
The full Thai name begins 'Krung Thep Maha Nakhon' ('City of Angels')
The Grand Palace complex, home to the Emerald Buddha, has been the royal residence since 1782
One of the world's most visited cities, with ~25 million international visitors annually
Known for its ornate Buddhist temples, floating markets, and street food culture

items.prompt: What is the capital of Vietnam?
items.explanation:
Population ~8.4 million (metro area); located in northern Vietnam on the Red River
Ho Chi Minh City (formerly Saigon) in the south is larger (~9 million) but is not the capital
Hanoi has been a capital for over 1,000 years, since 1010 under the Ly dynasty
The Old Quarter's 36 streets are each named for the goods traditionally sold there
Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body is displayed in a mausoleum in Ba Dinh Square, modeled on Lenin's tomb

items.prompt: What is the capital of Pakistan?
items.explanation:
Population ~1.2 million; a planned city that became the capital in 1967, replacing Karachi
Located at the base of the Margalla Hills in the Pothohar Plateau
Faisal Mosque (completed 1986) is one of the largest mosques in the world, designed by Turkish architect Vedat Dalokay
Karachi (~15 million) remains the largest city and economic hub
The name means 'City of Islam' — the city was built from scratch in the 1960s

items.prompt: What is the capital of Iran?
items.explanation:
Population ~9 million (city), ~16 million (metro area) — the largest city in Western Asia
Became the capital in 1786 under the Qajar dynasty, replacing Isfahan
Sits at the foot of the Alborz Mountains; Mount Damavand (5,671m) is visible on clear days
The 1979 Iranian Revolution and the 444-day U.S. Embassy hostage crisis both centered in Tehran
Golestan Palace, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is the former Qajar royal complex

items.prompt: What does Israel designate as its capital?
items.explanation:
Population ~950,000; sacred to Judaism, Christianity, and Islam
Israel declared Jerusalem its capital in 1950; the 1980 Jerusalem Law formalized the claim
Most countries maintain embassies in Tel Aviv; the U.S. moved its embassy to Jerusalem in 2018
The Old City contains the Western Wall, Church of the Holy Sepulchre, and Dome of the Rock within ~1 km
Jerusalem's status is one of the most contested issues in international diplomacy

items.prompt: What is the capital of Ukraine?
items.explanation:
Population ~2.9 million; one of Europe's oldest cities, founded in the 5th century
'Kiev' is the Russian-language spelling; 'Kyiv' (from Ukrainian) became standard internationally after 2018
The Kyiv Pechersk Lavra (Monastery of the Caves), founded 1051, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Saint Sophia Cathedral (built 1037) contains mosaics and frescoes from the original construction
Has been under military threat since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022

items.prompt: What is the capital of Poland?
items.explanation:
Population ~1.8 million; 85% of the city was destroyed in WWII, then meticulously rebuilt
The Old Town reconstruction was so faithful it became a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Became the capital in 1596, replacing Kraków when King Sigismund III moved the court
The Warsaw Uprising (August–October 1944) against Nazi occupation killed ~200,000 civilians
Chopin was born near Warsaw and his heart is preserved in a pillar at the Holy Cross Church

items.prompt: What is the capital of the Netherlands?
items.explanation:
Population ~900,000 (city), ~2.5 million (metro area); built on a network of 165 canals
Amsterdam is the constitutional capital, but the government sits in The Hague (Den Haag)
The Anne Frank House and the Rijksmuseum (home to Rembrandt's *Night Watch*) are top landmarks
The canal ring district is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
A common trivia follow-up: 'Where is the Dutch government seated?' — The Hague

items.prompt: What is the capital of Switzerland?
items.explanation:
Population ~135,000; technically designated the 'Federal City' rather than official capital
A common trivia trick — many guess Zurich (financial center) or Geneva (UN and Red Cross headquarters)
The Zytglogge, a medieval clock tower, is the city's most famous landmark
Albert Einstein developed his special theory of relativity while working at the patent office in Bern (1905)
Bern's Old Town, built on a peninsula in the Aare River, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site

items.prompt: What is the capital of Greece?
items.explanation:
Population ~3.2 million (metro area); continuously inhabited for over 5,000 years
Birthplace of democracy, Western philosophy, and the Olympic Games
The Acropolis and Parthenon (447–432 BCE) dominate the skyline
Hosted the first modern Olympic Games in 1896 and again in 2004
Named after the goddess Athena, who won patronage of the city over Poseidon in Greek myth

items.prompt: What is the capital of Sweden?
items.explanation:
Population ~1 million (city), ~2.4 million (metro area); spread across 14 islands
The Nobel Prize ceremonies are held at Stockholm's Concert Hall and City Hall each December 10
Gamla Stan (Old Town) dates to the 13th century and contains the Royal Palace
The Vasa Museum houses a nearly intact 17th-century warship that sank on its maiden voyage in 1628
Often called the 'Venice of the North' (a title shared with several other cities)

items.prompt: What is the capital of Norway?
items.explanation:
Population ~700,000; founded c. 1040 CE
Was named Christiania (later Kristiania) from 1624 to 1925 when it reverted to Oslo
The Nobel Peace Prize is awarded in Oslo (all other Nobels are awarded in Stockholm)
Home to the Viking Ship Museum and Edvard Munch's *The Scream* at the National Museum
One of the most expensive cities in the world, fueled by Norway's sovereign wealth fund (oil revenues)

items.prompt: What is the capital of Colombia?
items.explanation:
Population ~7.4 million; sits at 2,640 meters (8,660 ft) in the Andes — one of the highest major capitals
Founded in 1538 by the Spanish conquistador Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada
The Gold Museum (Museo del Oro) holds the world's largest collection of pre-Columbian gold artifacts
Colombia is frequently misspelled as 'Columbia' — a trivia pet peeve
Bogotá's TransMilenio bus rapid transit system is one of the world's largest

items.prompt: What is the capital of Peru?
items.explanation:
Population ~10.8 million (metro area); the largest city on South America's Pacific coast
Founded by Francisco Pizarro in 1535; the historic center is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Lima's food scene is world-renowned — Central restaurant has repeatedly been ranked among the world's best
Almost never rains (annual rainfall ~15mm) despite high humidity; sits in a coastal desert
The catacombs beneath the Basilica of San Francisco hold the bones of an estimated 25,000 people
